title: Preterm Labour
oneLiner: Uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks; leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Placental abruption** – sudden-onset severe abdominal pain + uterine hypertonicity (woody hard) + vaginal bleeding (may be concealed) + fetal distress (CTG late decelerations/tachycardia). *Single finding: uterine tenderness with high-resistance pattern on CTG.*
- **Chorioamnionitis** – maternal fever ≥38°C + fetal tachycardia >160 bpm + foul-smelling/cloudy liquor (if membranes ruptured). *Single finding: maternal pyrexia + fetal tachycardia unresponsive to steroids/fluid.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Regular, painful uterine contractions (≥4 in 20 min)
  - Lower back pain or pelvic pressure
  - Vaginal discharge (blood-stained, watery, or increased volume)
  - “Show” (mucous plug) or vaginal bleeding
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cervical dilatation ≥2 cm and effacement ≥80% on speculum/digital VE
  - Uterine tenderness or rigidity (suspect abruption)
  - Membranes may be ruptured (pooling, nitrazine, ferning)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Braxton Hicks contractions** – irregular, non-progressive, no cervical change. *Splitter: painless contractions without cervical effacement/dilatation.*
- **Preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM)** – history of gush of fluid, pooling on speculum. *Splitter: positive Amnisure/pooling without contractions → PPROM, not labour.*
- **Placental abruption** – vaginal bleeding (dark/clotted) + uterine tenderness + fetal distress. *Splitter: rigid uterus + fetal bradycardia/late decels ≠ routine labour.*
- **Urinary tract infection** – dysuria, loin tenderness, pyuria. *Splitter: positive urine dip without cervical change.*
- **Acute cervicitis/vaginitis** – discharge, no progressive contractions. *Splitter: no cervical effacement on serial exam.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - CTG – evaluate fetal heart rate pattern and contractions
  - Speculum examination – visualise cervix, check for membrane rupture (pooling, ferning, pH)
  - Urine dipstick – exclude UTI
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFTs, group & save (cross-match if abruption)
- **Imaging:**
  - Abdominal U/S – confirm presentation, EFW, liquor volume, placental position
  - Transvaginal cervical length – <25 mm predicts preterm birth
- **Special tests:**
  - Fetal fibronectin – if cervical length 20-30 mm (negative rule-out strong)
  - Amnisure/Amnicator kit – for ROM when ambiguous
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Regular contractions + cervical change (≥2 cm, ≥80%) OR cervical length <25 mm
  - NICE: treat as preterm labour if ≥2 cm dilated or ≥80% effaced
- **Never-miss test:** **CTG** + **speculum to exclude ROM** – missing chorioamnionitis or abruption is catastrophic.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Transfer to labour ward with NICU capability
  - Start IV access, cross-match if bleeding
  - CTG monitoring
  - Give **corticosteroids** (betamethasone 12 mg IM stat, repeat in 24 h) if 24+0 to 33+6 weeks (consider up to 35+6)
  - Administer **magnesium sulphate** for neuroprotection if <32 weeks (loading dose, then infusion)
  - **Tocolysis** (atosiban or nifedipine) – only if benefit of 48 h steroid window, and no contraindications (abruption, chorioamnionitis, severe pre-eclampsia)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue CTG monitoring for initial 12 h
  - If PPROM: prophylactic erythromycin (250 mg QDS for 10 days) – [check local policy for co-amoxiclav if infection suspected]
  - Avoid repeated vaginal exams unless labour progresses
  - Maternal observations 4-hourly, treat pyrexia
  - If no labour after 48 h, consider discharge with safety netting
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up in preterm surveillance clinic
  - Screen for cervical insufficiency (serial ultrasound) if recurrent
  - Progesterone therapy (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix (<25 mm) – [check local policy]
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s 125 mL/hr (if NBM)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily) if not bleeding and platelet count normal
  - Diet: light/sips; NBM if emergency delivery anticipated
  - Observations: BP, HR, temp, respiratory rate, SaO₂ 4-hourly; CTG 12 hourly initially
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Corticosteroids:** Betamethasone 12 mg IM, repeat in 24 h (single course only; rescue dose may be considered >2 weeks later if <34 weeks).
- **Magnesium sulphate (neuroprotection):** 4 g IV over 15-30 min, then 1 g/hour for 24 h (or until birth). Monitor for toxicity: loss of patellar reflex, respiratory depression.
- **Tocolytics:**
  - Atosiban: 6.75 mg IV bolus over 1 min, then 18 mg/hour IV infusion for 3 hours, then 6 mg/hour for up to 45 h.
  - Nifedipine: 10 mg oral every 4-6 hours (max 40 mg/day). **Do not use with magnesium due to risk of hypotension/respiratory depression.** [Check local policy for preferred agent]
- **Antibiotics (PPROM):** Erythromycin 250 mg QDS orally for 10 days (NICE). Add co-amoxiclav 375 mg TDS if vaginal GBS or clinical suspicion? [Check local policy]
- **GBS prophylaxis (if labour established):** Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV stat, then 1.5 g IV QDS. If allergic: clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS.
- **Key contraindications:** Tocolysis – chorioamnionitis, abruption, fetal distress, severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Magnesium – reduce infusion if creatinine >130 µmol/L or urine output <30 mL/hr. Atosiban – no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Magnesium sulphate – do not give IV bolus without checking respiratory rate and reflexes; overdose causes respiratory arrest.** Also, **nifedipine can cause profound hypotension if given with magnesium.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Neonatal: RDS, IVH, necrotising enterocolitis, sepsis
  - Maternal: chorioamnionitis, postpartum haemorrhage, retained placenta
- **Chronic:**
  - Neonatal: cerebral palsy, chronic lung disease, neurodevelopmental delay
  - Maternal: psychological trauma, recurrent preterm birth
sections.Prognosis: - Survival and serious morbidity depend on gestational age: >34 weeks survival >98% with minimal disability; 28-32 weeks 90-95% survival; <26 weeks high morbidity/mortality. Perinatal death occurs in 1-5% of non-malformed cases <32 weeks.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always give steroids even if delivery appears imminent** – benefits begin within 1-2 hours (reduced RDS, IVH).
- **Use magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if <32 weeks** – reduces risk of cerebral palsy regardless of delivery time.
- **Consider tocolysis only to allow steroid completion** – do not continue beyond 48 h unless transfer pending.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give tocolysis if chorioamnionitis or abruption** – delays proper treatment and worsens outcomes.
- **Do not repeat vaginal exams after rupture unless labour progressing** – increases infection risk.
- **Do not omit GBS prophylaxis** – even if patient is “allergic” – use clindamycin or check local protocol.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** (1) Worsening pain or regular contractions (>4 in 20 min) (2) Vaginal bleeding or leaking fluid (3) Fever or rigors (4) Reduced fetal movements.
- **Follow-up:** Antenatal clinic within 1 week; if PPROM, weekly CRP/CTG until 34 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/consultant immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Maternal fever >38°C unresponsive to paracetamol
  - Fetal heart rate abnormalities (persistent bradycardia <110 or tachycardia >160, late decelerations)
  - Vaginal bleeding heavy or with pain (suspect abruption)
  - Reduced fetal movements or abnormal CTG (reduce variability)
- **ICU/CCOT:** evidence of sepsis (lactate >2, organ dysfunction), respiratory depression (magnesium toxicity), or major haemorrhage.

rawMarkdown:
## Preterm Labour  
**One-liner:** Uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks; leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Placental abruption** – sudden-onset severe abdominal pain + uterine hypertonicity (woody hard) + vaginal bleeding (may be concealed) + fetal distress (CTG late decelerations/tachycardia). *Single finding: uterine tenderness with high-resistance pattern on CTG.*
- **Chorioamnionitis** – maternal fever ≥38°C + fetal tachycardia >160 bpm + foul-smelling/cloudy liquor (if membranes ruptured). *Single finding: maternal pyrexia + fetal tachycardia unresponsive to steroids/fluid.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Regular, painful uterine contractions (≥4 in 20 min)
  - Lower back pain or pelvic pressure
  - Vaginal discharge (blood-stained, watery, or increased volume)
  - “Show” (mucous plug) or vaginal bleeding
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cervical dilatation ≥2 cm and effacement ≥80% on speculum/digital VE
  - Uterine tenderness or rigidity (suspect abruption)
  - Membranes may be ruptured (pooling, nitrazine, ferning)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Braxton Hicks contractions** – irregular, non-progressive, no cervical change. *Splitter: painless contractions without cervical effacement/dilatation.*
- **Preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM)** – history of gush of fluid, pooling on speculum. *Splitter: positive Amnisure/pooling without contractions → PPROM, not labour.*
- **Placental abruption** – vaginal bleeding (dark/clotted) + uterine tenderness + fetal distress. *Splitter: rigid uterus + fetal bradycardia/late decels ≠ routine labour.*
- **Urinary tract infection** – dysuria, loin tenderness, pyuria. *Splitter: positive urine dip without cervical change.*
- **Acute cervicitis/vaginitis** – discharge, no progressive contractions. *Splitter: no cervical effacement on serial exam.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - CTG – evaluate fetal heart rate pattern and contractions
  - Speculum examination – visualise cervix, check for membrane rupture (pooling, ferning, pH)
  - Urine dipstick – exclude UTI
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFTs, group & save (cross-match if abruption)
- **Imaging:**
  - Abdominal U/S – confirm presentation, EFW, liquor volume, placental position
  - Transvaginal cervical length – <25 mm predicts preterm birth
- **Special tests:**
  - Fetal fibronectin – if cervical length 20-30 mm (negative rule-out strong)
  - Amnisure/Amnicator kit – for ROM when ambiguous
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Regular contractions + cervical change (≥2 cm, ≥80%) OR cervical length <25 mm
  - NICE: treat as preterm labour if ≥2 cm dilated or ≥80% effaced
- **Never-miss test:** **CTG** + **speculum to exclude ROM** – missing chorioamnionitis or abruption is catastrophic.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Transfer to labour ward with NICU capability
  - Start IV access, cross-match if bleeding
  - CTG monitoring
  - Give **corticosteroids** (betamethasone 12 mg IM stat, repeat in 24 h) if 24+0 to 33+6 weeks (consider up to 35+6)
  - Administer **magnesium sulphate** for neuroprotection if <32 weeks (loading dose, then infusion)
  - **Tocolysis** (atosiban or nifedipine) – only if benefit of 48 h steroid window, and no contraindications (abruption, chorioamnionitis, severe pre-eclampsia)
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**
  - Continue CTG monitoring for initial 12 h
  - If PPROM: prophylactic erythromycin (250 mg QDS for 10 days) – [check local policy for co-amoxiclav if infection suspected]
  - Avoid repeated vaginal exams unless labour progresses
  - Maternal observations 4-hourly, treat pyrexia
  - If no labour after 48 h, consider discharge with safety netting
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow-up in preterm surveillance clinic
  - Screen for cervical insufficiency (serial ultrasound) if recurrent
  - Progesterone therapy (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix (<25 mm) – [check local policy]
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s 125 mL/hr (if NBM)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg daily) if not bleeding and platelet count normal
  - Diet: light/sips; NBM if emergency delivery anticipated
  - Observations: BP, HR, temp, respiratory rate, SaO₂ 4-hourly; CTG 12 hourly initially

### Drugs & Doses
- **Corticosteroids:** Betamethasone 12 mg IM, repeat in 24 h (single course only; rescue dose may be considered >2 weeks later if <34 weeks).
- **Magnesium sulphate (neuroprotection):** 4 g IV over 15-30 min, then 1 g/hour for 24 h (or until birth). Monitor for toxicity: loss of patellar reflex, respiratory depression.
- **Tocolytics:**
  - Atosiban: 6.75 mg IV bolus over 1 min, then 18 mg/hour IV infusion for 3 hours, then 6 mg/hour for up to 45 h.
  - Nifedipine: 10 mg oral every 4-6 hours (max 40 mg/day). **Do not use with magnesium due to risk of hypotension/respiratory depression.** [Check local policy for preferred agent]
- **Antibiotics (PPROM):** Erythromycin 250 mg QDS orally for 10 days (NICE). Add co-amoxiclav 375 mg TDS if vaginal GBS or clinical suspicion? [Check local policy]
- **GBS prophylaxis (if labour established):** Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV stat, then 1.5 g IV QDS. If allergic: clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS.
- **Key contraindications:** Tocolysis – chorioamnionitis, abruption, fetal distress, severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Magnesium – reduce infusion if creatinine >130 µmol/L or urine output <30 mL/hr. Atosiban – no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Magnesium sulphate – do not give IV bolus without checking respiratory rate and reflexes; overdose causes respiratory arrest.** Also, **nifedipine can cause profound hypotension if given with magnesium.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Neonatal: RDS, IVH, necrotising enterocolitis, sepsis
  - Maternal: chorioamnionitis, postpartum haemorrhage, retained placenta
- **Chronic:**
  - Neonatal: cerebral palsy, chronic lung disease, neurodevelopmental delay
  - Maternal: psychological trauma, recurrent preterm birth

### Prognosis
- Survival and serious morbidity depend on gestational age: >34 weeks survival >98% with minimal disability; 28-32 weeks 90-95% survival; <26 weeks high morbidity/mortality. Perinatal death occurs in 1-5% of non-malformed cases <32 weeks.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always give steroids even if delivery appears imminent** – benefits begin within 1-2 hours (reduced RDS, IVH).
- **Use magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if <32 weeks** – reduces risk of cerebral palsy regardless of delivery time.
- **Consider tocolysis only to allow steroid completion** – do not continue beyond 48 h unless transfer pending.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give tocolysis if chorioamnionitis or abruption** – delays proper treatment and worsens outcomes.
- **Do not repeat vaginal exams after rupture unless labour progressing** – increases infection risk.
- **Do not omit GBS prophylaxis** – even if patient is “allergic” – use clindamycin or check local protocol.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** (1) Worsening pain or regular contractions (>4 in 20 min) (2) Vaginal bleeding or leaking fluid (3) Fever or rigors (4) Reduced fetal movements.
- **Follow-up:** Antenatal clinic within 1 week; if PPROM, weekly CRP/CTG until 34 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/consultant immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Maternal fever >38°C unresponsive to paracetamol
  - Fetal heart rate abnormalities (persistent bradycardia <110 or tachycardia >160, late decelerations)
  - Vaginal bleeding heavy or with pain (suspect abruption)
  - Reduced fetal movements or abnormal CTG (reduce variability)
- **ICU/CCOT:** evidence of sepsis (lactate >2, organ dysfunction), respiratory depression (magnesium toxicity), or major haemorrhage.
